content.problemStatement:
This year's annual budget involved a discussion on NNN sectors where the ithi^{th}ith sector was initially allocated an amount AiA_iAi​.

It was later realised that a minimum budget of XXX is required by each sector.

Allocation can be transferred from sector iii to any sector jjj only if the final allocation for sector iii remains **at least** XXX.

Find the **maximum** number of sectors that can meet the minimum required budget of XXX after possible transfers.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and XXX — the number of sectors and the minimum budget requirement of each sector, respectively.
The next line consists of NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…ANA_1, A_2, \ldots A_NA1​,A2​,…AN​, denoting the initial budget allocated to each sector.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **maximum** number of sectors that can meet the minimum required budget of XXX after possible transfers.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and X — the number of sectors and the minimum required budget per sector. The next line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN, the initial budget of each sector.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line — the maximum possible number of sectors that can have at least X budget after allowed transfers.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: sectors with budgets [2,1,3] and X=2. Sector 3 has 1 surplus unit, which can be given to sector 2, making all three sectors reach at least 2.
Test case 2: X=3 and no sector has surplus, so none can reach the required budget.
Test case 3: X=2, surplus=1 from sector 3, two sectors need 1 each; only one can be satisfied, giving a total of 3 sectors.
Good sectors: 5,6,10 (3 sectors). Surplus = (6‑5)+(10‑5)=6. Deficits: 4 needs 1, 3 needs 2. Both can be covered, so all 5 sectors meet the requirement.
All sectors are below X and there is no surplus, therefore no sector can reach the minimum budget.

solution.approach:
For each test case:
1. Count sectors that already have Ai ≥ X (they are already satisfied). For every such sector with Ai > X compute its surplus = Ai - X and add it to a total surplus variable.
2. For every sector with Ai < X compute its deficit = X - Ai and store these values in a list.
3. Sort the deficit list in non‑decreasing order. Greedily satisfy deficits from smallest to largest while the cumulative needed amount does not exceed the total surplus. The number of satisfied deficits is the number of additional sectors that can reach X.
4. Answer = (already satisfied sectors) + (number of satisfied deficits).
The greedy choice is optimal because satisfying a smaller deficit never harms the ability to satisfy any other deficit – it uses the least possible surplus for each additional sector.
Complexities: sorting dominates, giving O(N log N) time per test case and O(N) extra memory for the deficits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting the deficits).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) per test case for storing deficits.
